is an archaic [script style](/wiki/Script_style "Script style") of [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese"). It developed within the [state of Qin](/wiki/State_of_Qin "State of Qin") during the [Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Eastern_Zhou "Eastern Zhou") dynasty (771–256 BC), and was then promulgated across China in order to replace script varieties used in other [ancient Chinese states](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_states "Ancient Chinese states") following [Qin's wars of unification](/wiki/Qin%27s_wars_of_unification "Qin's wars of unification") and establishment of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC) under [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang"), the first [emperor of China](/wiki/Emperor_of_China "Emperor of China").

The **small seal script** is an archaic [script style](/wiki/Script_style "Script style") of [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese"). It developed within the [state of Qin](/wiki/State_of_Qin "State of Qin") during the [Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Eastern_Zhou "Eastern Zhou") dynasty (771–256 BC), and was then promulgated across China in order to replace script varieties used in other [ancient Chinese states](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_states "Ancient Chinese states") following [Qin's wars of unification](/wiki/Qin%27s_wars_of_unification "Qin's wars of unification") and establishment of the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC) under [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang"), the first [emperor of China](/wiki/Emperor_of_China "Emperor of China").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Small_seal_script&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

During the [Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Eastern_Zhou "Eastern Zhou") dynasty (c. 771 – 256 BC), local varieties of [Chinese character](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") forms had developed across the country, producing the 'scripts of the six states' (六國文字)—which were later collectively referred to as [large seal script](/wiki/Large_seal_script "Large seal script").[[1]](#cite_note-1) This variance was considered unacceptable by the rising [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC), who saw it as a hindrance to timely communication, trade, taxation, and transportation, as well as being a potential vector for fomenting political dissent.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Around 220 BC, [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") ordered a systematic standardization of the country's weights, measures, and currency, as well as its writing system. Character forms which differed from those used by Qin scribes were discarded, with the Qin forms becoming standard across the entire empire.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Standardization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Small_seal_script&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Standardization")]

The standardized use of small seal characters was promulgated via the *[Cangjiepian](/wiki/Cangjiepian "Cangjiepian")* primer compiled by Qin Shi Huang's ministers—namely his chancellor [Li Si](/wiki/Li_Si "Li Si"). This compilation, which was claimed to include 3,300 characters, is no longer extant, and is known only through Chinese commentaries over the centuries. Several hundred characters from fragmented commentaries were collected during the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") (1644–1912), and recent archeological excavations in [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") have uncovered several hundred more on bamboo strips, showing the order of the characters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the script found was not the small seal script, as the discovery dates back to the [Han period](/wiki/Han_period "Han period").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Encoding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Small_seal_script&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Encoding")]

The small seal script was initially proposed for inclusion in [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") in 2015. The 723-page proposal lists many of the best-known examples of Qing-era commentary images.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After several revisions of this proposal,[[5]](#cite_note-5) the Unicode Technical Committee accepted the script to be included in Unicode version 18.0 scheduled to appear in September 2026, using the name “Seal script”.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
